title: Policy-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning Model for De Novo RNA Design
author: Sun, Yutong
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: RNA sequence design aims to design RNA sequences that adopt a desired structure, but current methods often depend on thermodynamic folding engines, large training datasets, or fixed secondary-structure assumptions. Here, we developed Perle, a reinforcement learning-based framework for RNA sequence design that learns directly from user-defined target constraints. Perle uses a lightweight neural generator to produce candidate RNA sequences from random latent vectors and optimizes them with rewards based on base-pair satisfaction and sequence diversity. This target-specific strategy allows Perle to explore multiple valid sequences for the same structural target without offline pre-training or folding-engine calls during optimization. We evaluated Perle on small PDB-derived RNAs, CASP15 RNA targets, RNA-protein complex examples, and the QT45 polymerase ribozyme. Perle fully satisfied target base-pair constraints in the small PDB and CASP15 benchmarks, achieved average RMSD values below 5 Å for nine of ten small RNAs, and obtained the lowest average RMSD in four of six CASP15 targets. In the QT45 test, using an AlphaFold 3-derived target reduced the average false-pair count from more than ten to two positions per sequence. These results suggest that Perle provides a fast, flexible, and sequence-diverse framework for computational RNA design and downstream structural screening.
